category_summary:
**GPT-5.4** adoption metrics dominated the day. **Greg Brockman** [revealed staggering numbers](/?date=2026-03-17&category=social#item-8765e6e0991a) — 5T tokens/day within a week, $1B annualized net-new revenue — while **Sam Altman** [declared all 'hardcore builders'](/?date=2026-03-17&category=social#item-510d991caab8) have switched to **Codex**, signaling OpenAI's strongest API launch ever.

- **Mistral AI** CEO **Arthur Mensch** [announced a founding partnership](/?date=2026-03-17&category=social#item-979680b48705) with **NVIDIA's Nemotron Coalition** to co-develop frontier open-source base models, a major GTC highlight
- **Scobleizer's** viral demo of **Grok** [cataloging an entire expo hall video](/?date=2026-03-17&category=social#item-011cff84e255) (55M views) showcased practical multimodal capabilities; **xAI** also [launched a new **Text-to-Speech API**](/?date=2026-03-17&category=social#item-18652fa80b84)
- **Andrew Ng** [introduced agent-to-agent knowledge sharing](/?date=2026-03-17&category=social#item-dc89a9032f36) in his Context Hub tool, proposing a 'Stack Overflow for AI agents' concept
- **Perplexity** [shipped browser-agent control](/?date=2026-03-17&category=social#item-2909d5bd0da9) for **Comet**, letting AI take full control of logged-in sessions without connectors
- **Clément Delangue** (HuggingFace) [pushed back on cost narratives](/?date=2026-03-17&category=social#item-12740e0eaed8) with concrete data: fine-tuning under $2K, most real-world AI far cheaper than frontier training
- A **Google Gemini** team member [admitted they 'undersold' 1M context](/?date=2026-03-17&category=social#item-575012352616) performance, drawing massive engagement (4.5K likes)
- **Simon Willison** [published a deep-dive chapter](/?date=2026-03-17&category=social#item-a58db4d1a7f2) on agentic engineering patterns explaining how coding agents actually work under the hood

items.title:
Should there be a Stack Overflow for AI coding agents to share learnings with each other?

Last week...
items.content:
Should there be a Stack Overflow for AI coding agents to share learnings with each other?

Last week I announced Context Hub (chub), an open CLI tool that gives coding agents up-to-date API documentation. Since then, our GitHub repo has gained over 6K stars, and we've scaled from under 100 to over 1000 API documents, thanks to community contributions and a new agentic document writer. Thank you to everyone supporting Context Hub!

OpenClaw and Moltbook showed that agents can use social media built for them to share information. In our new chub release, agents can share feedback on documentation — what worked, what didn't, what's missing. This feedback helps refine the docs for everyone, with safeguards for privacy and security.

We're still early in building this out. You can find details and configuration options in the GitHub repo. Install chub as follows, and prompt your coding agent to use it:

npm install -g @aisuite/chub

GitHub: https://t.co/OCkyxXQMCq

items.title:
They tell you training and running AI model costs billions.

That's true for a few frontier labs. Bu...
items.content:
They tell you training and running AI model costs billions.

That's true for a few frontier labs. But for most real-world use cases? Dramatically lower than you think thanks to open-source.

Real examples from @HuggingFace's latest analysis:
- Fine-tune a text classification model: <$2k
- Train a leading image embedding model: < $7k
- Train Deepseek OCR: < $100k
- Train a leading machine translation model: <$500k

Compare that to GPT-4.5 training (~$300M est.)

And the truth is that you don't need a Formula 1 car to pick up groceries. Most tasks are solved just as well by smaller, efficient, targeted models. The mistake everyone makes? Starting with "what's the best AI model?" instead of "what do I need to do?"

The future of AI is not just bigger models. It's cheaper, more customized, open models solving specific problems.

Explore 100+ real model costs of training and deployment yourself in the study!

items.title: P-EAGLE from @AmazonScience and @NVIDIAAIDev removes the sequential bottleneck in speculative decodi...
items.content:
P-EAGLE from @AmazonScience and @NVIDIAAIDev removes the sequential bottleneck in speculative decoding — all K draft tokens generated in a single forward pass.

📈 Up to 1.69x speedup over vanilla EAGLE-3 on NVIDIA B200, with 5-25% gains sustained at high concurrency (c=64).

How it works: EAGLE drafts tokens autoregressively (K tokens = K forward passes). P-EAGLE replaces this with parallel generation using learned mask tokens and shared hidden states — one pass, K tokens.

Pre-trained P-EAGLE heads available on @huggingface for GPT-OSS 120B, GPT-OSS 20B, and Qwen3-Coder 30B. Enable with one config change:
`--speculative-config '{"method": "eagle3", "model": "amazon/GPT-OSS-20B-P-EAGLE", "num_speculative_tokens": 7, "parallel_drafting": true}'`

Integrated in vLLM since v0.16.0.

Blog: https://t.co/0Oh7fWAV4g

🤝 Thanks to the @amazon and @NVIDIAAI teams for the contribution!

items.reasoning: Significant technical advancement in inference optimization. P-EAGLE removes sequential bottleneck in speculative decoding with concrete speedup numbers, integrated into vLLM v0.16.0. High engagement from technical community and practically deployable.

items.title:
You can now run a 1 trillion parameter model on your desk.

NVIDIA just made its DGX Station availab...
items.content:
You can now run a 1 trillion parameter model on your desk.

NVIDIA just made its DGX Station available to order from OEMs. It's a desktop supercomputer powered by the GB300 Grace Blackwell Ultra Superchip.

→ 20 petaflops of AI compute
→ 748GB of coherent memory
→ Fits data-center-class AI on your desk
→ ~$100K price tag

The hardware fuses a 72-core Arm CPU and a Blackwell Ultra GPU via NVLink, sharing one giant memory pool.

No data transfer bottleneck between CPU and GPU.

Paired with NemoClaw, an open-source stack, you spin up a sandboxed, always-on AI agent locally with one command.

Your code, your data, never leaves the machine.
https://t.co/bwu4Yw0ZNS

items.title: Someone used Suno AI to generate a Japanese metal band called Neon Oni. Fake member bios, AI-generat...
items.content:
Someone used Suno AI to generate a Japanese metal band called Neon Oni. Fake member bios, AI-generated music videos, "Based in Tokyo" on Spotify.

80,000+ monthly listeners. Fans had it in their Spotify Wrapped top 5. Merch was selling.

Then, community sleuths exposed it. Traced the creator's account to Europe. Spotted AI-generated hands in the music videos.

The creator's response? Recruit 7 real musicians from actual Tokyo bands to perform the AI-generated songs live.

They've now played several live shows and have more on the books.

From an interview with the band's creator: "In an age where AI is taking everyone's jobs, this has actually created jobs. It's done the complete opposite."

The AI --> real band transformation is a wild one.
items.author: @TheRundownAI
items.summary: TheRundownAI tells the story of Neon Oni, a fake Japanese metal band created with Suno AI that gained 80K+ Spotify listeners before being exposed. The creator then hired real Tokyo musicians to perform the AI songs live.
items.reasoning: Viral post (3,706 likes, 1M+ views) about an fascinating real-world case study of AI-generated music crossing into reality. Raises important questions about AI authenticity, creative industry disruption, and novel business models.

items.title:
Open-source AI just got a serious power-up.

Mistral AI joins NVIDIA's Nemotron Coalition as a found...
items.content:
Open-source AI just got a serious power-up.

Mistral AI joins NVIDIA's Nemotron Coalition as a founding member, co-developing the base model behind Nemotron 4.

→ Trained on NVIDIA DGX Cloud, fully open-sourced
→ 10x faster inference vs previous-gen H200 hardware
→ MoE: 675B total params, only 41B active per query
→ Runs from data centers down to laptops

The trick is Mixture-of-Experts: instead of running the full model every time, only the relevant "expert" neurons fire.

Pair that with NVIDIA's hardware parallelism, and you get frontier performance at a fraction of the compute cost.

You can now fine-tune a frontier-class base model for your industry, your region, your use case, without touching a closed API.

https://t.co/DMRlOTFdnw

One of the flagship projects at FAIR was to "embed the world" (i.e. represent every entity on Facebook).
The name was soon changed to "Filament", deployed internally, and eventually open-sourced as "PyTorch-BigGraph"

items.title:
Stop building one agent to do everything. OpenAI just shipped subagents natively inside Codex.

One ...
items.content:
Stop building one agent to do everything. OpenAI just shipped subagents natively inside Codex.

One main agent can now spawn specialized child agents on demand.

→ Context window stays clean
→ Multiple parts of a task run at once
→ Steer each agent independently mid-task
→ Works in the app and CLI today

Under the hood, each subagent gets its own isolated thread. The orchestrator handles spawning, routing instructions, and collecting results.

You can now split a full-stack feature across agents the same way you'd split it across teammates.
https://t.co/1KCGNZ9bCa
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignalAI reports OpenAI shipped native subagents in Codex: main agent spawns specialized child agents with isolated threads, parallel task execution, and independent steering.

→ Context window stays clean

→ Works in the app and CLI today

items.title: TLDR: Thinking open models will win on absolute performance is more than just cope, it's holding the...
items.content:
TLDR: Thinking open models will win on absolute performance is more than just cope, it's holding the open ecosystem from building into something that's different, better, and far more influential at the frontier of AI.

Eg Open models can be tools and compliment the best agents.
items.author: @natolambert
items.summary: Nathan Lambert argues that expecting open models to win on absolute performance is counterproductive - open models should instead serve as tools that complement the best agents, building a differentiated ecosystem rather than chasing frontier performance.
items.reasoning: Strategic thesis from a respected AI researcher about the future of open-source AI. Reframes the open vs closed debate productively. Good engagement (12K views, 118 likes).
items.summary_html: Nathan Lambert argues that expecting open models to win on absolute performance is counterproductive - open models should instead serve as tools that complement the best agents, building a differentiated ecosystem rather than chasing frontier performance.
items.content_html:
TLDR: Thinking open models will win on absolute performance is more than just cope, it's holding the open ecosystem from building into something that's different, better, and far more influential at the frontier of AI.

Eg Open models can be tools and compliment the best agents.

items.content: I am not sure "Forward Deployed AI Engineers" are going to deliver on what a lot of companies are hoping for. They are useful, yes, but AI applications are far less of a technical issue, and much more about rethinking the deep expertise & structure of your organization around AI.

items.title: Today at GTC, we announced that we’re building NVIDIA ecosystem-native models and an optimized insta...
items.content:
Today at GTC, we announced that we’re building NVIDIA ecosystem-native models and an optimized instance of our agentic North platform to meet the growing demand for secure, privately deployed AI systems alongside @nvidia.

These custom models will be optimized for NVIDIA’s latest architecture and software ecosystem, including NVIDIA DGX Spark – bringing a high-performance, locally run enterprise AI workstation to Spark owners and customers.

Learn more on our blog, and visit us at the GTC South Market Lot, Booth #7027: https://t.co/TpqkAmiAk3 

#NVIDIAGTC

items.title:
Multimodal OCR

Parse Anything from Documents

On document parsing benchmarks, it ranks second only ...
items.content:
Multimodal OCR

Parse Anything from Documents

On document parsing benchmarks, it ranks second only to Gemini 3 Pro on our OCR Arena Elo leaderboard, surpasses existing open-source document parsing systems, and sets a new state of the art of 83.9 on olmOCR Bench. On structured graphics parsing, dots.mocr achieves higher reconstruction quality than Gemini 3 Pro across image-to-SVG benchmarks, demonstrating strong performance on charts, UI layouts, scientific figures, and chemical diagrams

items.title:
xAI just shipped a TTS API that laughs, whispers, and sighs on command.

Grok's Text-to-Speech API t...
items.content:
xAI just shipped a TTS API that laughs, whispers, and sighs on command.

Grok's Text-to-Speech API turns text into expressive audio with a single POST call.

→ 5 distinct voices, built-in
→ Inline tags: laughs, whispers, pauses, emphasis
→ 20+ auto-detected languages
→ From telephony (8kHz) to studio-grade (48kHz)

The whole voice stack was built in-house: VAD, tokenizer, and audio models trained from scratch.

Building a voice app no longer means stitching three separate APIs together.
https://t.co/KFnsVkp0k3

Crazy so I think they essentially built consistent img2img of game frames into AI image models to make it photorealistic at 60 FPS

You could have a very basic rudimentary 3d scene in games and just let AI finish it off with a prompt and some media assets of the characters to make them consistent

This considering 3d gaming itself was forever stuck in the uncanny valley of not fully realistic until we got AI image models now to fix that

Very cool

items.title: I think an interesting mental model to understand the social, scientific and cultural impact of vide...
items.content:
I think an interesting mental model to understand the social, scientific and cultural impact of video/world models is to see the evolution of cameras as their precursor.

Early cameras often borrowed from the logic of the stage. They recorded performances, scenes, spectacles. Then artists took the camera and made moving pictures. Then cinema. Cinema did not simply become television, but it created the mass grammar of edited moving images. Television brought that grammar into the home. Streaming made it on demand. YouTube made it native to the internet. Short-form platforms like TikTok compressed it even further. Each step created new economies, new audiences, new grammars of expression. Meanwhile, cameras kept shrinking, spreading, multiplying. We embedded them everywhere. Phones, cars, doorbells, drones, satellites, telescopes, medical devices. New forms of art and science. Through all of it, the underlying act was the same: capturing reality and making it reproducible.

The camera captures reality. Video/world models, this new kind of camera, simulates reality.

At first, generative video mostly felt like a tool for experimental artists. It was the most obvious way to see strange new things move on a screen. Over time, it became an answer to shots that were impossible, impractical, or prohibitively expensive. But it did not stay there. Advertising agencies now use it to prototype and produce commercials. Game developers use it for world design. Architects use it for spatial visualization. Educators use it to explain complex systems. Robotics teams use it for policy learning and synthetic data. Autonomous vehicle companies use it to test edge cases that cannot be safely staged in the real world. Video models are growing, spreading, multiplying. Frame by frame, they infer structure, motion, lighting, causality, and interaction. This allows them not just to generate frames, but to simulate future states of a world. Video models become world models.

Cameras multiplied images, and images changed culture and scientific practice. Simulation multiplies possible realities and in return simulation is changing art and science.
items.author: @c_valenzuelab
items.summary: Cristóbal Valenzuela (Runway CEO) shares long essay on video/world models as the evolution of cameras - from capturing reality to simulating reality, with applications across advertising, gaming, architecture, robotics, and autonomous vehicles
items.reasoning: Thoughtful long-form essay from the CEO of Runway on the cultural and scientific significance of video/world models. Well-articulated framework comparing camera evolution to generative video evolution. Lower engagement than expected for the depth.
items.summary_html: Cristóbal Valenzuela (Runway CEO) shares long essay on video/world models as the evolution of cameras - from capturing reality to simulating reality, with applications across advertising, gaming, architecture, robotics, and autonomous vehicles

items.title:
A surgeon just removed a man's prostate ...and he was 1,500 miles away.

From an office in London, D...
items.content:
A surgeon just removed a man's prostate ...and he was 1,500 miles away.

From an office in London, Dr. Prokar Dasgupta controlled a 4-armed robot at a hospital in Spain.

The robot was fitted with a 3D camera and connected to London via fiber optic cable with a backup 5G connection (lag was just 0.06 seconds).

The story is wild:

> 62-year-old Paul Buxton had expected to fly to England and wait months on the NHS list.

> Instead, the surgeon came to him... through a fiber optic cable.

> Dasgupta is already scheduled to perform the procedure again on March 14th, with around 20,000 surgeons watching live.

Access to care is going to look so different in the future.

For context, in many low-income countries, there are fewer than 1 trained surgeon per 100,000 people.

The US alone is projected to face a shortage of up to 20,000 surgical specialists by 2036.

...Remote surgery doesn't just make medical care more convenient, it may be the only way some patients ever receive it one day.
items.author: @rowancheung
items.summary: Rowan Cheung covers a remote surgery milestone: a surgeon in London removed a prostate via robot from 1,500 miles away in Spain, with only 0.06s lag. Highlights global surgical shortage context.

items.title: Stop setting up connectors and MCPs. Perplexity's browser agent can now access any site you're alrea...
items.content:
Stop setting up connectors and MCPs. Perplexity's browser agent can now access any site you're already logged into.

Perplexity just shipped Computer inside Comet, its Chromium-based AI browser. A browser agent that operates the web on your behalf.

> No API integrations needed
> Works on any site you're logged into
> Fully local, your cookies stay on your machine
> You see every action in real time

The key insight: instead of building connectors to every service, the agent just uses your local browser session the same way you would.

It sees what you see, clicks what you'd click.

This matters because the bottleneck for web automation was always authentication and integration setup. That bottleneck is now gone.

You can point it at your Gmail, your dashboards, your internal tools, and it executes. No setup. No OAuth flows.

No glue code.
https://t.co/GzYHePw0FF

items.title:
Stop running your AI agent in the cloud. It now lives directly on your machine.

Manus just launched...
items.content:
Stop running your AI agent in the cloud. It now lives directly on your machine.

Manus just launched a desktop app with a feature called My Computer, a local AI agent with full access to your computer's resources.

> Sorts thousands of photos automatically
> Renames hundreds of files in bulk
> Builds Swift desktop apps, zero manual code
> Runs scheduled tasks and local workflows

The shift is architectural. Before, the agent ran in a sandboxed cloud environment. Now it runs locally, meaning it can read your files, launch apps, and execute system-level actions without sending data to a remote server.

This unlocks something concrete: you can now automate tasks that touch private or local data, think invoice processing, personal file management, or native app development, without your files ever leaving your machine.

Works on macOS and Windows today.
https://t.co/LB2KsyVL8O

items.title:
Residual connections just got a 90-degree rotation — and it changes how deep networks learn.

https:...
items.content:
Residual connections just got a 90-degree rotation — and it changes how deep networks learn.

https://t.co/fYXIemK5se introduced Attention Residuals (AttnRes): a new technique replacing fixed skip connections with learned, input-dependent attention across previous layers.

> 1.25x compute efficiency at scale
> Works on a 48B param sparse model
> Under 2% added inference latency
> Cleaner gradients across all depths

Every layer in a deep network adds a fixed copy of the previous state — no filtering, no selection.

Over time, signals dilute and gradients become uneven. AttnRes replaces that fixed addition with a soft attention mechanism: the network learns which past layer to pull from, not just the last one.

You can now swap residual connections in existing architectures without redesigning them. Training becomes more stable, representations stay sharp deeper in the network, and you spend 20% less compute to reach the same performance.

https://t.co/1CRtDDFLww

items.summary: AlphaSignalAI covers Attention Residuals (AttnRes) research: replacing fixed skip connections with learned, input-dependent attention across previous layers. 1.25x compute efficiency, tested at 48B params.

items.title: @pbeisel Actually, fast-following is a profitable business strategy as long as you are fast and you ...
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer gives a lengthy analysis of NVIDIA's competitive position at GTC, praising Jensen Huang's leadership, calling NVIDIA and Tesla 'entertainment' while saying everyone else is 'House of Pain.' Discusses fast-following as a business strategy.
items.reasoning: Substantive industry analysis from a known tech commentator reporting live from GTC. Moderate engagement. Provides color on NVIDIA's dominance narrative.
items.summary_html: Scobleizer gives a lengthy analysis of NVIDIA's competitive position at GTC, praising Jensen Huang's leadership, calling NVIDIA and Tesla 'entertainment' while saying everyone else is 'House of Pain.' Discusses fast-following as a business strategy.
items.content_html:
@pbeisel Actually, fast-following is a profitable business strategy as long as you are fast and you are good at following fast. But most aren't fast, and they're not good at following fast, so generally that strategy doesn't work because your company usually sucks. But if your company rocks and you're following fast, then maybe.

NVIDIA is an amazing company. You see it and you feel it here at GTC; they're leading in a whole lot of places. It's an interesting battle. There is so much happening here and so much happening in the AI space. NVIDIA is an innovative company run by an amazing founder who fills hockey stadiums with AI nerds in a way that very few other people can.

I'd be worried about everybody else. NVIDIA and Tesla are entertainment; everybody else is "House of Pain," as Jim Cramer would say.

items.title: @iqbalbhawana There are two levers here in the Gemini API: billing account caps and user set spend c...
items.author: @OfficialLoganK
items.summary: Logan from Google provides detailed explanation of Gemini API billing tiers, spending caps, and how they work including the 10-minute latency
items.reasoning: Detailed first-party information about Gemini API billing structure, tier system, and spending caps. Useful for developers building on Gemini.
items.summary_html: Logan from Google provides detailed explanation of Gemini API billing tiers, spending caps, and how they work including the 10-minute latency
items.content_html:
@iqbalbhawana There are two levers here in the Gemini API: billing account caps and user set spend caps.

For billing account caps, starting in April, each Tier (1-3) will have a max spending cap set. Once you hit that limit, you cannot spend more (upper bound of spending).

For user set spend caps, you can set a cap of $10 for example on Tier 1 (this is what my cap is on my personal account). Once you hit the cap, we will start rejecting all future API requests until the first day of the next month when it resets. Note there is a 10 minute latency in when the cap is hit and when we start rejecting requests, and customers are responsible for the overages here.

items.title:
Researchers from several universities proposed AgentOS: A new operating system architecture.  

AI a...
items.content:
Researchers from several universities proposed AgentOS: A new operating system architecture.  

AI agents replace traditional systems like Windows, macOS, or Linux. 

> No more app switching
> One natural language interface
> Agents coordinate everything automatically
> Your OS learns your habits over time 

The key insight: current AI agents are powerful but still run inside traditional OS.

It introduces an "Agent Kernel" at the core. Instead of launching apps, you describe what you want. 

The kernel breaks it down, picks the right skill modules, and dispatches specialized agents to execute each step. 

Apps become modular skills. 

Say "analyze this report and send slides to my team." The kernel picks the right skills, chains them, and executes
 
You can now describe a full workflow in one sentence and have the computer figure out the rest.

items.title: 🏆The Kaggle Book — Master Data Analysis and #DataScience Competitions with #MachineLearning, GenAI, ...
items.content:
🏆The Kaggle Book — Master Data Analysis and #DataScience Competitions with #MachineLearning, GenAI, and LLMs [2nd Edition]: https://t.co/hrVstwy4zH v/ @PacktDataML

Table of Contents:
🔶Introducing Data Science Competition
🔷Organizing Data with Datasets
🔶Work & Learn with Kaggle Notebooks
🔷Kaggle Models
🔶Leveraging Discussion Forums
🔷Detailing Competition Tasks & Metrics
🔶Designing Good Validation Schemes
🔷Modeling for Tabular Competitions
🔶Hyperparameter Optimization
🔷Ensembling & Stacking Solutions
🔶Modeling Image Classification & Segmentation

My Review (on Amazon):
This 700-page masterpiece of writing covers everything you need—start to finish—to be a competitive coder, specifically for Kaggle data science competitions. The book covers the mechanics of the competitions (platform, resources, rankings, leaderboards), then the infrastructure (notebooks, GitHub, data sets, frameworks, discussion forums), and then nearly 500 pages devoted to "Elevating Your Game" (in-depth coverage of modeling techniques, evaluation metrics, validation strategies, hyperparameter optimization, ensembles, stacking, and various categories of competitions: tabular data, computer vision, NLP, Gen AI, simulations). The book concludes with a valuable section on building your Kaggle portfolio for career advancement and new opportunities. This is an outstanding data science / AI / Machine Learning training resource for anyone, even if you are not into the competitions, though especially if you are a dedicated Kaggler.

items.title: Stop building stream tools from scratch. Lovable just gave every streamer a full Twitch dev stack vi...
items.content:
Stop building stream tools from scratch. Lovable just gave every streamer a full Twitch dev stack via prompt.

Lovable added a native Twitch connector. Connect your account, describe what you want, get a working app.

> Stream overlays, live
> Subscriber alert systems
> Viewer-controlled game mechanics
> Channel analytics dashboards

The hard part was always auth. Lovable's gateway handles OAuth 2.0 automatically, tokens refresh in the background, and credentials never touch your project code.

You describe the tool in plain English, the platform wires it to Twitch's Helix API, and you get a deployable app.

No OAuth setup, no token management, no boilerplate.

Building custom streaming software used to mean weeks of API docs and auth headaches. Now a working overlay or moderation tool is one prompt away, and the same workflow applies to any other service you want

to plug in.
https://t.co/vDh02XPIYL

items.title:
If your Tailscale is hacked

The hacker now has direct access to your server

But now he still needs...
items.content:
If your Tailscale is hacked

The hacker now has direct access to your server

But now he still needs to get into your SSH with an SSH key

So to get in two extremely rare things have to happen:
1) Tailscale is hacked
2) There's an SSH 0-day that they can use to hack into your server

That's still superior to NOT using Tailscale where they'd only need:
1) There's an SSH 0-day that they can use to hack into your server

It's like saying "why do you use an alarm system for your house, what if it breaks?"
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio makes a layered security argument for Tailscale: even if hacked, an attacker still needs an SSH 0-day, making it strictly better than no Tailscale where only the SSH 0-day is needed.
items.reasoning: Well-articulated security argument with strong engagement (89K views). Not AI-specific but popular developer security discussion from an influential indie hacker.
items.summary_html: Levelsio makes a layered security argument for Tailscale: even if hacked, an attacker still needs an SSH 0-day, making it strictly better than no Tailscale where only the SSH 0-day is needed.

items.title:
The bigger opportunity is APIs.

We saw 4G enable Uber, Grubhub, the app economy.

Now 5G + network ...
items.content:
The bigger opportunity is APIs.

We saw 4G enable Uber, Grubhub, the app economy.

Now 5G + network APIs enable developers to build applications that assume guaranteed performance.

That is a fundamental shift.

When performance becomes programmable, telcos move up the value chain.

From connectivity providers → to experience platforms.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon

items.title:
False

Many examples of SSH access 0-days and hacks

SSH should never be exposed to the entire inter...
items.content:
False

Many examples of SSH access 0-days and hacks

SSH should never be exposed to the entire internet

SSH is like your front door, even if you are the only one with the key, your lock might have a production defect (very rare but it happens)

So you gotta have a fence around your house too

But actually Tailscale is more like an airborne concrete tunnel from wherever you are in the world (on your laptop) all the way to your server's front door

https://t.co/FVtRD7KYby
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio argues SSH should never be exposed to the entire internet due to potential 0-day vulnerabilities, using an analogy of Tailscale as a concrete tunnel from your laptop to your server.
items.reasoning: Part of his server security thread. Well-articulated security practice but not AI-related.
items.summary_html: Levelsio argues SSH should never be exposed to the entire internet due to potential 0-day vulnerabilities, using an analogy of Tailscale as a concrete tunnel from your laptop to your server.
items.content_html:
False

Many examples of SSH access 0-days and hacks

SSH should never be exposed to the entire internet

SSH is like your front door, even if you are the only one with the key, your lock might have a production defect (very rare but it happens)

So you gotta have a fence around your house too

But actually Tailscale is more like an airborne concrete tunnel from wherever you are in the world (on your laptop) all the way to your server's front door

https://t.co/FVtRD7KYby

items.title:
My takeaway:

Premium connectivity will redefine ARPU, but only if product and marketing teams stop ...
items.content:
My takeaway:

Premium connectivity will redefine ARPU, but only if product and marketing teams stop selling speed and start selling experiences.

The demand is already there.

If 60% of customers will pay for performance at an event, what other moments are we under-monetizing?

Watch the full conversation to see how this plays out in 2026, and what it means for your strategy.

MIT: 35 Best Courses in Machine Learning!  @MIT

Explore a world of knowledge with free online courses from MIT on edX, featuring lessons in AI, machine learning, computer science engineering, circuits and electronics, Genetics, data science, statistics and much more.  Many people are unaware that edX hosts an incredible collection of free online courses from some of the top educational institutions globally. You can dive into everything from AI to Python programming without any cost. A significant number of these courses are crafted by MIT experts.  We highly encourage you to take advantage of this opportunity, and to kickstart your learning journey, here’s a curated selection of the best free online courses from MIT that you can explore this month:

items.title:
5G network slicing is the unlock.

It allows operators to create purpose-built slices of the network...
items.content:
5G network slicing is the unlock.

It allows operators to create purpose-built slices of the network for specific use cases:

→ Ultra-low latency for gaming
→ High uplink quality for live streaming
→ Secure, fraud-protected transactions for retail
→ Immersive AR and VR experiences

This is not theoretical.

Operators in Asia are already monetizing event-based passes, four-day upgrades, even influencer packages.

items.title:
We are watching a shift from selling bandwidth → to selling outcomes.

Speed is table stakes.

Perfo...
items.content:
We are watching a shift from selling bandwidth → to selling outcomes.

Speed is table stakes.

Performance is premium.

Think about a World Cup stadium:

→ Ordering food from your seat
→ Streaming live as an influencer
→ Placing secure mobile payments
→ Playing real-time AR experiences

In those moments, latency and reliability matter more than raw Mbps.

That is where differentiated connectivity comes in.

items.title: @aakashgupta I was in high school when he did this. I never saw it back then. Why? We didn't have in...
items.content:
@aakashgupta I was in high school when he did this. I never saw it back then. Why? We didn't have internet back then. My friend started a BBS about that time that could only handle text files. No YouTube. No X. No TikTok. No Instagram. 

That's why last week was the first time I had seen this. Very special talk too.

items.title: The best Brutalist/Brutalist-inspired architecture of the 1960s through 1980s still looks more like ...
items.content:
The best Brutalist/Brutalist-inspired architecture of the 1960s through 1980s still looks more like the future than almost anything built before or since.

items.title: Even bigger irony of getting rich is that everything expensive isn't that much better than when you ...
items.content:
Even bigger irony of getting rich is that everything expensive isn't that much better than when you paid normal for it

Many things are even worse (most expensive luxury hotels are guaranteed worse than regular simple hotels, I know I tried most of them now)

The real reason you wanna get rich is not to buy expensive things

It's so that $1M invested gives you 3% to take out every year with no risk, which is $30,000/year 

Which you can use to travel for $1000/mo on a shoestring budget forever without having to back to some desk job with a shitty boss

Aka FREEDOM

The next day we had freezing weather and snow (my backyard p...
items.content:
Last week, one day it was 84°F (29 C).

The next day we had freezing weather and snow (my backyard photo below).

Today we expect warm, severe weather (possible tornadoes — weather alert below). 

Tomorrow morning: freezing temperatures again.

We are living in interesting times.
— https://t.co/WDVkJZE4od

items.title:
The irony is that traveling on <$1000/mo is way more fun than >$10,000/mo

Luxury travel is extremel...
items.content:
The irony is that traveling on <$1000/mo is way more fun than >$10,000/mo

Luxury travel is extremely boring, comfortable, not challenging, sycophantic (yes sir)

Travel on a shoestring budget you get inventive, are forced to meet locals just to survive and get around, have to hitchhike etc

I like to combine cheap and luxury travel which keeps my brain from decaying and the contrast actually lets you enjoy both
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio contrasts budget and luxury travel, arguing budget travel is more fulfilling and challenging.

I like to combine cheap and luxury travel which keeps my brain from decaying and the contrast actually lets you enjoy both
